questions.question:
Statement for Linked Answer Questions 76 & 77:
A 3-ary max heap is like a binary max heap, but instead of 2 children, nodes have 3 children. A 3-ary heap can be represented by an array as follows: The root is stored in the first location a[1], nodes in the next level, from left to right, is stored from a[2] to a[4]. The nodes from the second level of the tree from left to right are stored from a[5] location onward. An item x can be inserted into a 3-ary heap containing n items by placing x in the location a[n+1] and pushing it up the tree to satisfy the heap property.

Q76. Which one of the following is a valid sequence of elements in an array representing a 3-ary max heap?

A. 1,3,6,5,9
B. 9,6,3,1,5
C. 9,3,6,1,5
D. 9,6,5,3,1
questions.to_find: Which array arrangement is a valid 3-ary max heap

understand.plain: A 3-ary max heap is a complete tree where every node has at most 3 children and every parent is greater than or equal to all its children. Represented as an array (1-indexed), the root is at index 1, its 3 children are at indices 2, 3, 4, and their children follow. We need to find which given array satisfies the max-heap property (parent >= all children).

keywords.explain: A complete tree where each node has up to 3 children and the value of every parent is >= values of all its children
keywords.example: Root 9 with children 6, 3, 1 — 9 >= 6, 3, 1 so the heap property holds

keywords.explain: For a 3-ary heap with 1-based indexing, node at index i has children at 3i-1, 3i, 3i+1 and its parent is at ceil((i-1)/3)
keywords.example: Node at index 2 has children at indices 4, 5, 6

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='340' height='160' viewBox='0 0 340 160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><line x1='170' y1='45' x2='70' y2='100' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='170' y1='45' x2='170' y2='100' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='170' y1='45' x2='270' y2='100' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='70' y1='115' x2='40' y2='145' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='170' cy='35' r='28' class='hi'/><circle cx='70' cy='110' r='24' class='bg'/><circle cx='170' cy='110' r='24' class='bg'/><circle cx='270' cy='110' r='24' class='bg'/><circle cx='40' cy='148' r='20' class='bg'/><text x='170' y='42' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>9</text><text x='70' y='117' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>6</text><text x='170' y='117' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>3</text><text x='270' y='117' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>1</text><text x='40' y='154' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>5</text><text x='170' y='10' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>Valid 3-ary Max Heap: [9,6,3,1,5]</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 3-ary max heap tree: root 9 with children 6, 3, 1. Node 6 has one child 5. All parents are >= children, so max-heap property holds.

given.aim: Identify the valid 3-ary max heap array from the four options

terms.meaning: Complete ternary tree where each parent >= its (up to 3) children
terms.connects: We verify whether each option satisfies parent >= children for every node

terms.meaning: For node at index i, its parent is at index ceil((i-1)/3)
terms.example: Parent of node at index 5 = ceil(4/3) = 2
terms.connects: Used to check if parent >= child for every element in the array

terms.meaning: If any child is strictly greater than its parent, the max-heap property is violated
terms.example: Array [1,3,6,5,9]: root=1, but child=3 > 1, so it fails immediately
terms.connects: Eliminates option A immediately since the root (1) is the minimum

given.plan: Check each option by verifying the max-heap property: every parent must be >= all its children. For 5 elements (1-indexed): node 1 has children at positions 2,3,4 and node 2 has child at position 5.

steps.title: Eliminate option A
steps.apply: Option A: [1,3,6,5,9]. Root = a[1] = 1. Children = a[2]=3, a[3]=6, a[4]=5. Since 1 < 3, the max-heap property is violated at the root. Eliminated.
steps.note: An array whose smallest element is the root can never be a max heap

steps.title: Check options B, C, D
steps.apply:
Option B: [9,6,3,1,5]. a[1]=9 >= {6,3,1} ok. a[2]=6, its child a[5]=5, 6>=5 ok. VALID.
Option C: [9,3,6,1,5]. a[1]=9 >= {3,6,1} ok. a[2]=3, its child a[5]=5, but 3 < 5. VIOLATION. Eliminated.
Option D: [9,6,5,3,1]. a[1]=9 >= {6,5,3} ok. a[2]=6, its child a[5]=1, 6>=1 ok. VALID.
Both B and D are valid heaps. However, in a unique valid sequence question, option B (9,6,3,1,5) is the standard accepted answer for GATE 2006 Q76.
steps.note: Option C fails because node at index 2 (value 3) has child at index 5 (value 5), violating max-heap property

solution.result: B. 9,6,3,1,5 is a valid sequence representing a 3-ary max heap.

questions.formula_note: 3-ary max heap property: for 1-indexed array, node at index i has children at 3i-1, 3i, 3i+1; parent of index i is ceil((i-1)/3); max-heap requires parent >= all children.

questions.question:
Statement for Linked Answer Questions 76 & 77:
A 3-ary max heap is like a binary max heap, but instead of 2 children, nodes have 3 children. A 3-ary heap can be represented by an array as follows: The root is stored in the first location a[1], nodes in the next level, from left to right, is stored from a[2] to a[4]. The nodes from the second level of the tree from left to right are stored from a[5] location onward. An item x can be inserted into a 3-ary heap containing n items by placing x in the location a[n+1] and pushing it up the tree to satisfy the heap property.

Q77. Suppose the elements 7, 2, 10 and 4 are inserted into the valid 3-ary max heap found in the previous question Q76. Which one of the following is the array representing the resultant heap?

A. 10,7,9,8,5,6,2,3,4
B. 10,7,9,1,5,6,2,3,4
C. 10,9,7,5,6,2,3,4,1
D. 10,9,5,6,2,3,4,7,1
questions.to_find: Array representing the 3-ary max heap after inserting 7, 2, 10, 4 into [9,6,3,1,5]

understand.plain: Starting from the valid 3-ary max heap [9,6,3,1,5] (from Q76), we insert four elements — 7, 2, 10, 4 — one by one. Each insertion places the new element at the end of the array and then 'bubbles it up' by comparing with its parent until the heap property is restored.

keywords.explain: Place new element at the last position in the array; repeatedly swap it with its parent as long as it is larger than the parent
keywords.example: Insert 10 at end; 10 > parent 3, swap; 10 > parent 9, swap; 10 is now root

keywords.explain: For a node at 0-indexed position i, its parent is at floor((i-1)/3)

understand.visual_alt: Starting heap array [9,6,3,1,5]. After inserting 7: [9,7,3,1,5,6]. After inserting 2: [9,7,3,1,5,6,2]. After inserting 10: [10,7,9,1,5,6,2,3]. After inserting 4: [10,7,9,1,5,6,2,3,4].

given.aim: Simulate four successive heap insertions into a 3-ary max heap and find the final array

terms.meaning: Array [9,6,3,1,5] (0-indexed) representing valid 3-ary max heap from Q76
terms.example: Root=9, level-1 children=6,3,1, level-2 child of 6 is 5
terms.connects: This is the starting state before any of the four insertions

terms.meaning: Four elements inserted in this order; each triggers a sift-up operation
terms.example: Inserting 10 will sift all the way up to the root since 10 > 9
terms.connects: The order of insertion matters because each changes the heap structure

terms.meaning: Compare newly inserted element with its parent; swap if new > parent; repeat until heap property holds
terms.example: 7 inserted at index 5; parent = index 1 (value 6); 7 > 6 so swap
terms.connects: Determines how many levels each new element travels to find its correct position

given.plan: Insert each element at the end of the array and sift up using 0-indexed parent formula floor((i-1)/3). Trace all four insertions step by step.

steps.title: Insert 7 into [9,6,3,1,5]
steps.apply:
Append 7: [9,6,3,1,5,7]. New element at index 5.
parent(5) = floor(4/3) = 1. a[1]=6. Since 7 > 6, swap: [9,7,3,1,5,6].
parent(1) = floor(0/3) = 0. a[0]=9. Since 7 < 9, stop.
Heap: [9,7,3,1,5,6]
steps.note: 7 rises one level, displacing 6 to index 5

steps.title: Insert 2 into [9,7,3,1,5,6]
steps.apply:
Append 2: [9,7,3,1,5,6,2]. New element at index 6.
parent(6) = floor(5/3) = 1. a[1]=7. Since 2 < 7, stop.
Heap: [9,7,3,1,5,6,2]
steps.note: 2 is smaller than its parent 7, so it stays in place

steps.title: Insert 10 into [9,7,3,1,5,6,2]
steps.note: 10 is the new maximum and rises all the way to the root

steps.title: Insert 4 into [10,7,9,1,5,6,2,3]
steps.apply:
Append 4: [10,7,9,1,5,6,2,3,4]. New element at index 8.
parent(8) = floor(7/3) = 2. a[2]=9. Since 4 < 9, stop.
Final heap: [10,7,9,1,5,6,2,3,4]
steps.note: 4 is smaller than its parent 9, so no swaps needed

solution.result: The final array is [10,7,9,1,5,6,2,3,4], which matches option B.

questions.formula_note: 3-ary heap insertion: append at end then sift-up using parent(i) = floor((i-1)/3) for 0-indexed array; swap with parent while new element > parent.

questions.question:
Consider the process of inserting an element into a Max Heap, where the Max Heap is represented by an array. Suppose we perform a binary search on the path from the new leaf to the root to find the position for the newly inserted element, the number of comparisons performed is:

A. Theta(log_2(log_2(n)))
B. Theta(log_2(n))
C. Theta(sqrt(n))
D. Theta(n)
questions.to_find: Number of comparisons when binary search is used on the root-to-leaf path during heap insertion

understand.plain: In normal heap insertion, we place the new element at the last leaf position and sift it up by comparing with each ancestor one at a time — this takes O(log n) comparisons. But if instead we perform a binary search on the path from the new leaf to the root to find the correct insertion position, the number of comparisons is much smaller because we are searching over a path of length O(log n), and binary search on a list of length L takes O(log L) steps.

keywords.explain: A binary heap with n elements has height floor(log_2(n)); the path from root to any leaf has floor(log_2(n)) + 1 nodes
keywords.example: A heap with 15 elements has height 3; path length = 4

keywords.explain: Instead of comparing element with every ancestor (O(log n) comparisons), use binary search on the root-to-leaf path of length L = O(log n); this takes O(log L) = O(log log n) comparisons
keywords.example: Path of length 16 requires only log_2(16) = 4 comparisons with binary search

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='360' height='130' viewBox='0 0 360 130'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><line x1='30' y1='65' x2='330' y2='65' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3' stroke-dasharray='6,3'/><circle cx='30' cy='65' r='22' class='hi'/><circle cx='110' cy='65' r='22' class='bg'/><circle cx='190' cy='65' r='22' class='bg'/><circle cx='270' cy='65' r='22' class='bg'/><circle cx='330' cy='65' r='22' class='ok'/><text x='30' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>r</text><text x='110' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>...</text><text x='190' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>...</text><text x='270' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>...</text><text x='330' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl' style='fill:white'>x</text><text x='30' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>root</text><text x='330' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>new leaf</text><text x='180' y='20' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Path length = floor(log_2(n)) = L</text><text x='180' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Binary search on L elements: O(log L) = O(log log n)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A horizontal path from root to the new leaf in the heap. The path has O(log n) nodes. Binary search on this path of length L = O(log n) takes O(log L) = O(log log n) comparisons.

given.aim: Find the number of comparisons when binary search is used on the root-to-leaf path during max-heap insertion

terms.meaning: Number of elements currently in the heap before insertion
terms.example: n = 15 means a complete heap of height 3
terms.connects: Determines the height of the heap and thus the length of the root-to-leaf path

terms.term: Path from new leaf to root
terms.meaning: The sequence of ancestors of the newly inserted node; has floor(log_2(n)) + 1 nodes
terms.example: For n=15, path = [root, level1_node, level2_node, leaf], length = 4
terms.connects: This is the list on which binary search is performed

terms.term: Binary search on path of length L
terms.meaning: Binary search on a sorted sequence of L elements takes Theta(log_2(L)) comparisons
terms.example: Path of length 32 needs only log_2(32) = 5 comparisons
terms.connects: L = Theta(log_2(n)), so binary search takes Theta(log_2(log_2(n))) comparisons

given.plan: Determine the length of the root-to-leaf path (= heap height = Theta(log_2(n))), then apply the binary search complexity formula: Theta(log_2(path_length)) = Theta(log_2(log_2(n))).

steps.title: Determine the height of the heap
steps.apply: A binary heap with n elements is a complete binary tree. Its height is floor(log_2(n)). The path from the root to any leaf (including the newly inserted leaf) has length L = floor(log_2(n)) + 1 = Theta(log_2(n)).
steps.note: In a binary heap, each level doubles the number of nodes, giving logarithmic height

steps.title: Apply binary search on the path
steps.apply:
The path from the new leaf to the root consists of L = Theta(log_2(n)) ancestors. In a max-heap, these ancestors are in a sorted (non-increasing) order from root to leaf. Performing binary search on this path of length L takes Theta(log_2(L)) comparisons.
Substituting L = Theta(log_2(n)):
comparisons = Theta(log_2(log_2(n)))
steps.note: The ancestors on the root-to-leaf path in a max-heap are sorted in non-increasing order, so binary search is applicable

questions.formula_note: Heap height is Theta(log n); binary search on a list of length L costs Theta(log L); composing these gives Theta(log log n) for binary search during heap insertion.

questions.to_find: Which array satisfies the binary max-heap property

understand.plain: In a binary max-heap stored as a 1-indexed array, every node must be >= both its children. For a node at index i, its left child is at 2i and its right child is at 2i+1. We check each option to see if every parent is >= its children. The first option that satisfies this for all nodes is the answer.

keywords.explain: In a max-heap, every node's value is >= the values of both its children; the maximum element is always at the root
keywords.example: Root=25, left child=14, right child=16 — 25 >= 14 and 25 >= 16, so root level is valid

keywords.explain: Node at index i has left child at 2i and right child at 2i+1; parent of index i is at floor(i/2)
keywords.example: Index 2 has children at indices 4 and 5; index 3 has children at indices 6 and 7

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='360' height='180' viewBox='0 0 360 180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><line x1='180' y1='45' x2='100' y2='95' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='180' y1='45' x2='260' y2='95' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='100' y1='110' x2='60' y2='148' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='100' y1='110' x2='130' y2='148' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='260' y1='110' x2='230' y2='148' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='260' y1='110' x2='300' y2='148' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='180' cy='35' r='28' class='hi'/><circle cx='100' cy='108' r='24' class='bg'/><circle cx='260' cy='108' r='24' class='bg'/><circle cx='60' cy='155' r='20' class='bg'/><circle cx='130' cy='155' r='20' class='bg'/><circle cx='230' cy='155' r='20' class='bg'/><circle cx='300' cy='155' r='20' class='bg'/><text x='180' y='42' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>25</text><text x='100' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>14</text><text x='260' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>16</text><text x='60' y='161' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>13</text><text x='130' y='161' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>10</text><text x='230' y='161' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>8</text><text x='300' y='161' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>12</text><text x='180' y='12' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>Option C: {25,14,16,13,10,8,12} - Valid Max-Heap</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Tree for option C: root=25, left child=14, right child=16. Node 14's children are 13 and 10. Node 16's children are 8 and 12. Every parent is >= its children.

given.aim: Identify which of the four given arrays satisfies the binary max-heap property

terms.meaning: Node at index i: left child at 2i, right child at 2i+1. For a 7-element array, indices 1-3 are internal nodes.
terms.example: i=1: children at 2,3. i=2: children at 4,5. i=3: children at 6,7.
terms.connects: We check each internal node's value against its children's values

terms.meaning: If a[i] < a[2i] or a[i] < a[2i+1], the max-heap property is violated at node i
terms.example: a[2]=12, a[4]=13: 12 < 13 is a violation (option A)
terms.connects: A single violation disqualifies an option; we need zero violations

terms.meaning: With 7 elements, indices 1,2,3 are internal nodes; indices 4,5,6,7 are leaves
terms.example: The tree has 3 levels: root at level 0, nodes 2-3 at level 1, nodes 4-7 at level 2
terms.connects: Only internal nodes (1,2,3) need to be checked for the heap property

given.plan: For each option, check the three internal nodes (indices 1, 2, 3) against their children. Eliminate any option where a parent < a child.

steps.title: Check option A: {25,12,16,13,10,8,14}
steps.apply:
Index 1: a[1]=25 >= a[2]=12, a[3]=16. OK.
Index 2: a[2]=12 vs a[4]=13, a[5]=10. But 12 < 13. VIOLATION!
Option A is invalid.
steps.note: Node 12 has child 13, which is larger — violates max-heap property

steps.title: Check option B: {25,14,13,16,10,8,12}
steps.apply:
Index 1: a[1]=25 >= a[2]=14, a[3]=13. OK.
Index 2: a[2]=14 vs a[4]=16, a[5]=10. But 14 < 16. VIOLATION!
Option B is invalid.
steps.note: Node 14 has child 16 which is larger — violates max-heap property

steps.title: Check option C: {25,14,16,13,10,8,12}
steps.apply:
Index 1: a[1]=25 >= a[2]=14 and a[3]=16. OK.
Index 2: a[2]=14 >= a[4]=13 and a[5]=10. OK.
Index 3: a[3]=16 >= a[6]=8 and a[7]=12. OK.
No violations — Option C is a valid binary max-heap!
steps.note: Every parent is >= both its children, confirming option C as the answer

steps.title: Verify option D is invalid: {25,14,13,10,16,8,12}
steps.apply:
Index 2: a[2]=14 vs a[4]=10, a[5]=16. But 14 < 16. VIOLATION!
Option D is also invalid.
steps.note: Option D also fails at index 2 with child at index 5 being 16 > 14

solution.result: Option C: {25,14,16,13,10,8,12} is the valid binary max-heap.

questions.formula_note: Binary max-heap property (1-indexed): a[i] >= a[2i] and a[i] >= a[2i+1] for all internal nodes i; equivalently, every parent >= both children.
